class_schedule.topic: Incident Response Frameworks: NIST and SANS
learning_objectives: Compare the NIST SP 800-61 and SANS incident response frameworks and their phase structures
Explain how regulatory requirements influence IR program design across industries
Describe the relationship between incident response, business continuity, and disaster recovery
Identify the triggers that escalate a security event to an incident
readings: NIST SP 800-61 Rev. 2: Computer Security Incident Handling Guide (complete)
SANS Incident Handler's Handbook
CISA Federal Incident Notification Guidelines

lecture_notes.introduction: Incident response is the organized approach to addressing and managing the aftermath of a security breach or cyberattack. The goal is to handle the situation in a way that limits damage, reduces recovery time and costs, and prevents recurrence. Without a structured framework, organizations respond reactively and inconsistently, making costly mistakes under pressure. The two dominant frameworks in the industry are NIST SP 800-61 and the SANS Incident Response Process. While both share the same fundamental concepts, they organize phases differently and emphasize different aspects. NIST defines four phases (Preparation, Detection and Analysis, Containment Eradication and Recovery, Post-Incident Activity), while SANS breaks these into six more granular steps (Preparation, Identification, Containment, Eradication, Recovery, Lessons Learned). Understanding both frameworks enables IR professionals to adapt to any organizational context. This course builds practical IR skills through progressively more complex exercises, culminating in a full tabletop exercise and IR plan capstone.
key_concepts: NIST SP 800-61 four-phase lifecycle: Preparation, Detection & Analysis, Containment/Eradication/Recovery, Post-Incident Activity
SANS six-step process: Preparation, Identification, Containment, Eradication, Recovery, Lessons Learned (PICERL mnemonic)
Event vs incident: an event is any observable occurrence; an incident is an event that violates security policy or threatens operations
Incident classification taxonomy: confidentiality breach, integrity violation, availability disruption, combined attacks
Severity classification: Critical (existential threat), High (major business impact), Medium (moderate impact), Low (minimal impact)
IR program maturity model: ad-hoc, repeatable, defined, managed, optimizing — assessing organizational readiness
Regulatory IR requirements: GDPR 72-hour notification, HIPAA breach notification, SEC 4-day material incident disclosure
IR program integration with business continuity (BC) and disaster recovery (DR) planning
Incident response retainers: pre-negotiated agreements with external IR firms for surge capacity
IR metrics: MTTD, MTTC, MTTR as baseline measurements for program effectiveness
lecture_notes.demonstration: Side-by-side walkthrough of how NIST and SANS frameworks would handle the same scenario: a ransomware infection detected on three endpoints. Mapping each response action to the corresponding framework phase, identifying where the frameworks overlap and where they diverge in emphasis.
setup_requirements: Sample IR plan from a Fortune 500 company (anonymized)

review.value: NIST combines containment, eradication, and recovery into a single phase, emphasizing their iterative nature. SANS separates them, providing clearer action boundaries for less experienced responders. Both are valid; choose based on organizational maturity and team experience.

A failed login attempt is an event. Ten thousand failed login attempts from a single IP in one minute is an incident. The threshold between event and incident is defined by organizational policy and should trigger specific response procedures.

GDPR requires notification to supervisory authority within 72 hours of becoming aware of a personal data breach. HIPAA requires notification within 60 days. SEC requires material cybersecurity incident disclosure within 4 business days. These deadlines drive IR timeline urgency.

Most organizations operate at level 1-2 (ad-hoc to repeatable). Level 3 (defined) requires documented procedures and trained staff. Level 4 (managed) adds metrics and continuous measurement. Level 5 (optimizing) uses metrics to drive continuous improvement.

learning_objectives: Design a CSIRT organizational structure appropriate for different organization sizes and types
Define roles, responsibilities, and authority levels for each IR team position
Develop escalation procedures and communication channels for incident response
Create an on-call rotation and notification system for 24/7 IR coverage
readings: NIST SP 800-61 Rev. 2 Ch. 2.4: Incident Response Team Structure
Building a World-Class Security Operations Center (MITRE publication)
FIRST CSIRT framework documentation

lecture_notes.introduction: An incident response capability is only as effective as the team executing it. The CSIRT (Computer Security Incident Response Team) must have clearly defined roles, appropriate authority to take containment actions, established communication channels, and access to the tools and information needed for rapid response. Team structure varies significantly by organization size: a small business may have a single IT person who handles security incidents alongside other duties, while a Fortune 500 company may have a 24/7 SOC feeding into a dedicated IR team with specialized forensic analysts, threat intelligence analysts, and malware reverse engineers. Regardless of size, certain roles must be filled: someone must have authority to make containment decisions (isolate a server, disable an account), someone must manage communications with stakeholders, and someone must perform the technical analysis. This session covers how to build and maintain an effective IR team, including cross-training, external partnerships, and the critical human factors that determine whether a team performs well under the extreme stress of a real incident.
key_concepts: CSIRT models: dedicated team, distributed team (members from other departments), hybrid model, virtual team, outsourced SOC
Core IR roles: IR Manager (authority and decisions), Triage Analyst (initial assessment), Forensic Analyst (deep technical analysis), Threat Intelligence Analyst (context and attribution)
Supporting roles: Legal counsel, HR, Communications/PR, Executive sponsor, IT operations liaison
Authority matrix: who can isolate a server, disable a user account, engage law enforcement, contact external IR firm, notify regulators
Escalation procedures: defined triggers for escalating from SOC analyst to IR team to CISO to CEO/Board
Communication channels: dedicated IR Slack channel, encrypted email, bridge line for real-time coordination
On-call rotation design: primary and secondary on-call, escalation after 15 minutes, rotation fairness, burnout prevention
Cross-training: ensuring no single point of failure where one person's absence cripples the IR capability
External partnerships: IR retainer firms, threat intelligence sharing (ISACs), law enforcement relationships
Stress management: recognizing cognitive fatigue during extended incidents, mandatory rest periods, psychological support
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building an IR team structure for a mid-sized financial services company (2000 employees): defining the team composition, creating an authority matrix, designing escalation procedures, and developing an on-call rotation. Students will receive a scenario where key team members are unavailable and must adapt the structure.

review.value: During a ransomware incident at 2 AM, the on-call analyst must know whether they can isolate an entire network segment without waiting for CISO approval. Ambiguous authority causes delays measured in hours during which damage compounds. Pre-authorize specific actions by severity level.

Distributed teams draw members from IT, security, legal, and communications who return to their normal roles between incidents. Cost-effective but slower to assemble. Dedicated teams are always ready but expensive. Most organizations use a hybrid: small dedicated core with distributed surge capacity.

Information Sharing and Analysis Centers (ISACs) provide sector-specific threat intelligence, early warnings, and peer collaboration. Financial services (FS-ISAC), healthcare (H-ISAC), and others share IoCs and TTPs among members. This collective defense is often the earliest warning of sector-targeted attacks.

Major incidents can last days or weeks with intense pressure and sleep deprivation. Mandatory 12-hour shifts with enforced rest periods, clear handoff procedures between shifts, and post-incident psychological support are essential. A fatigued analyst makes mistakes that extend the incident.

learning_objectives: Develop a comprehensive incident response plan covering all phases and incident types
Create communication templates for internal stakeholders, customers, regulators, and media
Design and maintain an IR toolkit with forensic tools, containment scripts, and documentation
Conduct IR readiness assessments to identify gaps before incidents occur
readings: NIST SP 800-61 Rev. 2 Ch. 3.1: Preparation
CISA Incident Response Plan Basics
Example IR plans from publicly available sources

lecture_notes.introduction: Preparation is the most important phase of incident response because it determines how effectively an organization can execute all subsequent phases. The time to figure out who to call, what tools to use, and how to communicate is not during a crisis at 3 AM. A comprehensive IR plan documents procedures for every anticipated incident type, pre-authorizes containment actions by severity level, establishes communication channels and templates, and ensures the technical tools are deployed and tested. Preparation also includes proactive measures that reduce the likelihood and impact of incidents: deploying detection capabilities (EDR, SIEM, NDR), hardening systems to reduce attack surface, ensuring backup integrity, and pre-negotiating relationships with external IR firms, legal counsel, and law enforcement. The IR plan must be a living document, tested through regular tabletop exercises and updated based on lessons learned. An untested plan provides false confidence — the first time your plan is executed should not be during a real incident.
key_concepts: IR plan components: purpose and scope, authority, incident classification, response procedures per incident type, communication plan, escalation matrix
Communication templates: initial notification (internal), customer breach notification, regulatory filing, press statement, law enforcement referral
IR toolkit: jump bag contents (forensic laptop, write-blockers, evidence bags), software toolkit (Velociraptor, SIFT, forensic images), documentation templates
Detection infrastructure: SIEM (Splunk/Elastic), EDR (CrowdStrike/Defender), NDR (Zeek/Suricata), email security gateway
Backup verification: regular restoration tests, offline/immutable backups for ransomware resilience, documented RPO/RTO
IR retainer agreements: pre-negotiated SOW with external IR firms, defined response SLAs, legal privilege considerations
Tabletop exercise program: quarterly exercises with progressive difficulty, executive participation, after-action reports
IR plan distribution: secure storage, offline copies, designated holders who can access during network outage
Legal preparation: engagement letter with outside counsel, privilege protocols for IR communications, evidence preservation guidelines
Threat intelligence integration: subscriptions, ISAC membership, automated IoC feeds into detection tools
lecture_notes.demonstration: Drafting key sections of an IR plan for a healthcare organization: creating the incident classification matrix, writing communication templates for a PHI breach notification (HIPAA-compliant), and assembling a virtual IR toolkit with pre-configured forensic VMs and containment scripts.

review.value: The IR plan should pre-authorize specific actions by severity level without requiring real-time executive approval. Example: for Critical severity, the IR lead is authorized to isolate network segments, disable compromised accounts, and engage the external IR retainer without waiting for CISO approval.

IR communications should be conducted under attorney-client privilege when possible. The IR retainer should be engaged through outside counsel, and reports labeled as privileged. This protects IR findings from discovery in litigation. Consult legal before sharing any IR findings externally.

Ransomware actors specifically target backup systems. Immutable backups (write-once, air-gapped, or cloud with object lock) cannot be encrypted by ransomware. Regular restoration testing verifies that backups are not only intact but actually restorable. An untested backup is not a backup.

NIST recommends testing the IR plan at least annually. Best practice is quarterly tabletop exercises covering different scenarios (ransomware, data breach, insider threat, DDoS). Each exercise should include a different combination of participants to build organizational muscle memory.

class_schedule.topic: Detection and Analysis: SIEM and EDR
learning_objectives: Configure SIEM correlation rules and detection logic for common attack patterns
Perform hypothesis-driven threat hunting using the MITRE ATT&CK framework
Triage security alerts to distinguish true positives from false positives efficiently
Integrate EDR telemetry with SIEM data for comprehensive detection coverage
readings: Blue Team Handbook: SOC, SIEM, and Threat Hunting Ch. 1-6
MITRE ATT&CK framework navigator documentation
Sigma rule specification and detection engineering guide

lecture_notes.introduction: Detection and analysis is where incidents are identified, validated, and initially characterized. The modern SOC relies on a layered detection architecture: SIEM platforms aggregate and correlate logs from across the enterprise, EDR agents provide detailed endpoint telemetry, NDR solutions monitor network traffic, and threat hunting teams proactively search for threats that evade automated detection. The challenge is not a lack of data but an overwhelming volume of alerts. The average SOC receives thousands of alerts per day, of which only a small fraction represent genuine threats. Effective detection requires well-tuned correlation rules that minimize false positives, structured triage processes that quickly categorize alerts, and threat hunting programs that find adversaries who have evaded rule-based detection. The MITRE ATT&CK framework provides the common language for mapping detection capabilities to adversary techniques, identifying coverage gaps, and prioritizing new detection development. This session builds practical detection engineering and threat hunting skills.
key_concepts: SIEM architecture: log collection (agents, syslog, API), normalization, enrichment, correlation, alerting, dashboarding
Detection engineering: writing Sigma rules for vendor-agnostic detection logic, converting to Splunk SPL or Elastic KQL
Sigma rule anatomy: logsource (product, category), detection (selection, filter, condition), level, tags (ATT&CK technique IDs)
Alert triage workflow: validate source, check context (user, time, system), classify (true positive, false positive, benign true positive)
EDR capabilities: process creation trees, file modifications, registry changes, network connections, memory inspection, live response
Threat hunting methodologies: hypothesis-driven (ATT&CK-based), IoC-driven (intelligence-fed), anomaly-driven (statistical deviation)
ATT&CK-based detection coverage mapping: plotting current detections against the ATT&CK matrix to identify gaps
Detection-as-code: managing detection rules in version control with CI/CD pipelines for testing and deployment
Alert fatigue: causes (noisy rules, lack of tuning) and mitigations (tuning cadence, suppression rules, analyst rotation)
Purple teaming: collaborative exercises where red team executes techniques while blue team validates detection coverage
lecture_notes.demonstration: Writing three Sigma detection rules: (1) Detecting PsExec lateral movement via Windows Security Event Log process creation. (2) Detecting PowerShell download cradle execution via script block logging. (3) Detecting credential dumping via LSASS process access. Converting each rule to Splunk SPL and testing against sample attack data.
setup_requirements: Splunk or Elastic SIEM instance with security log data
MITRE ATT&CK Navigator for coverage mapping
EDR console (CrowdStrike Falcon or Microsoft Defender) in demo mode
Sample attack telemetry data for rule testing

review.value: Sigma rules are written once in a vendor-agnostic YAML format and converted to any SIEM platform's query language. This enables sharing detection logic across organizations regardless of their SIEM vendor, and ensures detections survive SIEM platform migrations.

Start with an ATT&CK technique (e.g., T1053.005 Scheduled Task). Form a hypothesis: 'An attacker may have established persistence via a scheduled task.' Search for new or modified scheduled tasks, focusing on tasks running executables from unusual paths (AppData, Temp, ProgramData).

An alert that correctly fires on the behavior it detects, but the behavior is legitimate. Example: a detection rule for PowerShell Invoke-WebRequest firing on the IT team's legitimate software deployment script. These require tuning (allowlist the specific script) rather than dismissal.

Use ATT&CK Navigator to visualize which techniques have detection coverage (colored) versus which do not (blank). Prioritize new detection development for high-impact techniques that your adversaries commonly use (based on threat intelligence for your sector).

learning_objectives: Execute containment actions for different incident types while preserving forensic evidence
Implement network-based containment including VLAN isolation, DNS sinkholing, and firewall rules
Perform host-based containment using EDR isolation, account lockout, and process termination
Balance containment speed against evidence preservation and business continuity requirements
readings: Incident Response & Computer Forensics Ch. 7-8
SANS Incident Handler's Handbook: Containment Phase
CrowdStrike/Defender EDR containment capabilities documentation

lecture_notes.introduction: Containment is the critical phase where responders race to limit the damage an attacker can inflict while simultaneously preserving evidence for investigation and potential legal proceedings. The containment decision is one of the most consequential in incident response: act too slowly and the attacker achieves their objective; act too aggressively and you alert the attacker (causing them to accelerate destruction), destroy forensic evidence, or cause unnecessary business disruption. Effective containment requires a layered approach combining network-level controls (blocking communications), host-level controls (isolating systems), and identity-level controls (disabling compromised accounts). Short-term containment stops the immediate bleeding; long-term containment prepares for eradication by establishing a clean perimeter. The containment strategy must be adapted to the incident type: ransomware requires immediate network isolation to prevent spread, while a stealthy data exfiltration may call for covert monitoring to understand the full scope before tipping off the attacker. Every containment decision must be documented with the rationale, timestamp, and authorizing party.
key_concepts: Short-term containment: immediate actions to stop active damage — network isolation, account lockout, DNS sinkhole, firewall blocks
Long-term containment: sustained controls while preparing for eradication — clean system rebuild, enhanced monitoring, temporary network segmentation
Network containment: VLAN isolation (move compromised hosts to quarantine VLAN), firewall rule updates, DNS sinkholing C2 domains, null routing attacker IPs
Host containment via EDR: network isolation (host can only communicate with EDR cloud), process kill, registry modification lockdown, USB blocking
Identity containment: disabling compromised accounts, forced password reset, revoking API keys and tokens, disabling VPN access
DNS sinkhole: redirecting C2 domain resolution to an internal IP, severing C2 while maintaining network presence for monitoring
Evidence preservation during containment: capture memory before shutdown, collect volatile data before network isolation, document the pre-containment state
Containment for different incident types: ransomware (immediate isolation), APT (covert monitoring then coordinated takedown), insider threat (HR involvement, legal hold)
Communication during containment: updating stakeholders without broadcasting sensitive details over compromised channels
Containment documentation: who authorized the action, when, what specific action was taken, what systems were affected
lecture_notes.demonstration: Executing a containment workflow for a simulated lateral movement incident: (1) Detecting a compromised workstation beaconing to an external C2, (2) Capturing memory remotely before containment, (3) Isolating the host via EDR network containment, (4) Sinkholing the C2 domain to identify other infected hosts, (5) Blocking the attacker's lateral movement path via firewall rules, (6) Documenting every action with timestamps and authorization.
setup_requirements: Lab network with managed switches supporting VLAN changes
DNS server configured for sinkhole operations
EDR agent with network isolation capability
Memory acquisition tools for remote collection

review.value: For ransomware: overt containment (immediate isolation) is appropriate because the attacker has already revealed themselves. For APT: covert containment (DNS sinkhole, monitoring) may be preferred to understand the full scope before alerting the attacker. The wrong choice can cause the attacker to accelerate destructive actions.

Modern EDR platforms can isolate a host so it can only communicate with the EDR cloud management plane. This allows continued remote investigation (running commands, collecting artifacts) while preventing lateral movement or C2 communication. More surgical than physical network disconnection.

For each containment action, evaluate: (1) Will this stop the active damage? (2) Will this preserve or destroy evidence? (3) What business impact will this cause? (4) Will this alert the attacker? (5) Is this action reversible if we make the wrong call? Document the decision rationale.

If the attacker has compromised email or Slack, IR communications through those channels reveal the response strategy. Use out-of-band communication: personal phones, Signal, dedicated IR bridge lines, or in-person briefings for sensitive containment planning.

learning_objectives: Plan and execute systematic threat eradication across all compromised systems
Develop recovery sequences prioritized by business criticality and risk
Implement credential rotation and persistence mechanism removal
Verify successful eradication through enhanced monitoring during recovery
readings: NIST SP 800-61 Rev. 2 Ch. 3.3: Containment, Eradication, and Recovery
NIST SP 800-34: Contingency Planning Guide
CISA recovery best practices documentation

lecture_notes.introduction: Eradication is the process of completely removing the threat from the environment, and recovery restores business operations to their pre-incident state. Both phases must be thorough and verified: incomplete eradication is the most common cause of re-compromise, often occurring within days of the initial remediation. Sophisticated attackers plant multiple persistence mechanisms, understanding that defenders may find some but miss others. A successful eradication requires identifying every persistence mechanism (services, scheduled tasks, registry keys, web shells, authorized SSH keys, golden tickets, implanted accounts), every backdoor, and every modified system component. The fundamental decision is whether to clean compromised systems in place or rebuild from known-good media. Rebuilding is more certain but takes longer; cleaning is faster but risks missing hidden persistence. Recovery must be sequenced by business priority, with critical systems restored first and verified clean before reconnecting to the network. Enhanced monitoring during recovery watches for signs of re-compromise, which would indicate the eradication was incomplete.
key_concepts: Eradication checklist: malware removal, backdoor elimination, persistence mechanism removal, compromised credential reset, vulnerability patching
Persistence mechanism inventory: Windows services, scheduled tasks, registry Run keys, WMI subscriptions, startup folder items, DLL search order hijacking
Clean rebuild vs in-place remediation: rebuild is definitive but slow; remediation is faster but may miss hidden persistence. Decision depends on severity and system criticality
Credential rotation: domain admin passwords first, then all privileged accounts, then all user accounts. KRBTGT password reset (twice) if Golden Ticket suspected
Recovery sequencing: Active Directory/DNS first, then critical business applications, then user workstations, then non-critical systems
Backup restoration: verify backup integrity before restoring, scan restored data for malware, restore to clean infrastructure not compromised hardware
Enhanced monitoring during recovery: heightened SIEM alerting, EDR in block mode, honeypot deployment on restored segments
Recovery verification milestones: define success criteria for each system before declaring it recovered
Business continuity integration: RPO (maximum data loss), RTO (maximum downtime), communication to customers and partners
Re-compromise indicators: same malware variant reappearing, C2 beacons resuming, unexpected account activity post-recovery
lecture_notes.demonstration: Executing a structured eradication and recovery for a simulated APT incident: identifying all persistence mechanisms (registry Run key, scheduled task, web shell, implanted admin account), removing each one, rotating all domain credentials including KRBTGT, rebuilding the compromised web server from a golden image, and setting up enhanced monitoring alerts for re-compromise indicators.
setup_requirements: Lab environment with multiple compromised systems from previous exercises
Clean golden images for system rebuild
Active Directory environment for credential rotation exercise
Enhanced monitoring rule templates for recovery phase

review.value: If the attacker obtained the KRBTGT account hash, they can forge Golden Tickets granting access to anything in the domain. The KRBTGT password must be reset TWICE (Active Directory remembers the current and previous passwords). Allow replication to complete between resets. This invalidates all existing Kerberos tickets.

Rebuild from known-good media when: the system was deeply compromised (rootkit, bootkit), you cannot be confident all persistence is removed, or the system handles sensitive data. Remediate in place when: the compromise was limited and well-understood, the system cannot tolerate extended downtime, and you can verify removal of all malicious artifacts.

AD/DNS must be recovered first because all other systems depend on them. If AD is compromised and not rebuilt, every subsequently recovered system is at risk. Recovery priority: (1) Identity infrastructure (AD, DNS, PKI), (2) Security tools (EDR, SIEM), (3) Critical business applications, (4) User endpoints.

Deploy honeypot accounts, files, and services during recovery. If the attacker retained access and attempts to use the environment, they may interact with honeypots, providing immediate detection of incomplete eradication. Example: a fake admin account with alerting on any authentication attempt.

learning_objectives: Conduct effective blameless post-incident reviews that identify systemic failures
Calculate and interpret IR metrics including MTTD, MTTC, and MTTR
Produce incident reports tailored for technical, executive, and regulatory audiences
Derive actionable improvement items with assigned owners and deadlines
readings: NIST SP 800-61 Rev. 2 Ch. 3.4: Post-Incident Activity
Google SRE Book Ch. 15: Postmortem Culture
PagerDuty Incident Response documentation: Post-Mortems

lecture_notes.introduction: The post-incident phase is where organizations transform a painful experience into lasting improvement. Without structured lessons learned, organizations repeat the same mistakes and suffer the same types of incidents. The blameless postmortem methodology, pioneered by high-reliability industries and adopted by leading technology companies, focuses on systemic failures rather than individual blame. When people fear punishment for honest reporting, they hide information that is essential for understanding what went wrong. A blameless culture encourages responders to share exactly what happened, including their mistakes and uncertainties, enabling the organization to identify and fix the root causes. The postmortem produces two critical outputs: an incident report documenting what happened for stakeholders and regulatory requirements, and an action item list of specific improvements to prevent recurrence. These action items must be tracked, assigned to owners with deadlines, and verified for completion. The post-incident phase also establishes metrics that measure the IR program's effectiveness over time, providing data for continuous improvement and executive reporting.
key_concepts: Blameless postmortem methodology: focus on 'what failed?' not 'who failed?', encourage honest reporting, identify systemic causes
Postmortem structure: incident summary, timeline, root cause analysis, contributing factors, action items, what went well
Root cause analysis techniques: 5 Whys, fishbone diagram (Ishikawa), fault tree analysis
IR metrics: MTTD (first compromise to first detection), MTTC (detection to effective containment), MTTR (containment to full recovery)
Metric calculation from incident timeline: identifying the exact timestamps for compromise, detection, containment, and recovery
Incident report audiences: technical team (detailed), executive/board (business impact and risk), regulatory (compliance-focused), customer (transparent but measured)
Action item tracking: specific, assigned, time-bound improvements with verification criteria
Detection gap analysis: what detections should have fired but did not, what new detections are needed
Process gap analysis: where did procedures fail or not exist, what decisions were delayed by unclear authority
Trend analysis: tracking incident types, root causes, and metrics over time to identify patterns and measure improvement
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conducting a full blameless postmortem for the containment exercise from Week 5: building the incident timeline from logs and responder notes, performing 5 Whys root cause analysis, calculating MTTD/MTTC/MTTR, identifying three specific detection gaps, and drafting action items with owners and deadlines.

review.value: Why was the server compromised? Unpatched vulnerability. Why was it unpatched? Not in the patch management system. Why not? It was provisioned outside the standard process. Why? The team needed it urgently and bypassed the process. Why was bypassing possible? No technical controls enforcing the process. Root cause: lack of automated enforcement for asset onboarding.

MTTD = Time of first detection alert - Time of initial compromise. The compromise time comes from forensic analysis (earliest evidence of attacker activity). Detection time is the first SIEM alert or human observation. A long MTTD indicates detection gaps; the industry average for APT is still measured in months.

Bad: 'Improve detection capabilities.' Good: 'Deploy Sigma rule for T1053.005 (Scheduled Task persistence) to Splunk by March 15. Owner: Detection Engineering. Verification: rule fires on test data from ATT&CK emulation.' Every action item must be specific, assigned, time-bound, and verifiable.

Blameless means not punishing people for honest mistakes in complex systems. It does not mean ignoring negligence or malicious actions. If someone deliberately bypassed security controls or refused to follow documented procedures, that is an HR matter separate from the technical postmortem.

learning_objectives: Execute a structured response to a malware infection affecting multiple endpoints
Perform rapid triage to determine malware type, capabilities, and propagation mechanism
Coordinate containment actions across network, endpoint, and identity layers
Extract and distribute indicators of compromise for enterprise-wide detection
readings: CISA Malware Analysis Report best practices
MITRE ATT&CK Software reference for common malware families
AV vendor threat intelligence reports for current malware campaigns

lecture_notes.introduction: Malware incidents remain the most frequent type of cybersecurity incident organizations face. From commodity trojans delivered by phishing to sophisticated APT implants, the IR team must quickly determine what the malware does, how it spread, how many systems are infected, and how to contain and eradicate it. The malware IR workflow begins with detection (AV alert, EDR detection, user report), followed by rapid triage to characterize the threat: is this a banking trojan stealing credentials, ransomware preparing to encrypt, a worm propagating across the network, or an APT implant establishing persistence? The triage outcome drives the containment strategy. An information-stealing trojan on a single endpoint requires different containment than a worm actively propagating across the network. Key early actions include isolating infected hosts, identifying all compromised systems through IoC sweeps, preserving evidence for forensic analysis, and coordinating with threat intelligence teams to understand the threat actor's objectives. This session covers the complete malware IR lifecycle from detection through eradication.
key_concepts: Malware IR workflow: detect, triage (type/capabilities/scope), contain, analyze, eradicate, recover, lessons learned
Rapid triage process: hash lookup (VirusTotal), behavior assessment (EDR telemetry), initial scope (IoC sweep across enterprise)
Malware categorization for IR: data theft (credential stealing, exfiltration), destructive (ransomware, wiper), persistent (RAT, backdoor), propagating (worm, lateral movement)
IoC extraction from EDR: process hashes, file paths, registry modifications, network connections, parent process chains
Enterprise IoC sweep: deploying search across all endpoints for known indicators using EDR, SIEM queries, or Velociraptor
Network containment for malware: blocking C2 IPs/domains at firewall and proxy, DNS sinkholing, IDS/IPS signature deployment
Endpoint containment: EDR network isolation, process termination, autorun removal, quarantine of malicious files
Malware sample handling: secure extraction for analysis, uploading to private sandbox, sharing with AV vendors
Coordinated eradication: simultaneous cleanup across all identified infected hosts to prevent re-infection from missed hosts
Vaccination: deploying detection rules, blocking hashes, and patching the exploit vector before declaring recovery
lecture_notes.demonstration: Responding to a simulated Emotet-style malware outbreak: receiving the initial AV alert, analyzing EDR telemetry to identify the malware's behavior (document macro dropper, second-stage download, credential theft, lateral movement via SMB), extracting IoCs, sweeping the enterprise for additional infections, coordinating containment across 15 identified infected endpoints, and executing simultaneous eradication.
setup_requirements: Lab environment with multiple endpoints infected with simulated malware
EDR console with detection and isolation capabilities
SIEM with log data for IoC sweeping

review.value: The first minutes of a malware incident determine the outcome. Priority 1: Is it actively propagating? If yes, network containment immediately. Priority 2: Is it encrypting data? If yes, isolate affected systems and assess backup status. Priority 3: Is it exfiltrating data? If yes, block C2 communications. Character of the malware drives the response tempo.

Once you have IoCs from the initial infected host (file hashes, C2 IPs, registry keys), sweep the entire enterprise for the same indicators. Use EDR real-time search, SIEM historical query, and network monitoring. The initial detection usually represents a fraction of the total infection scope.

If you clean infected Host A but Host B is still infected, Host B will re-infect Host A. All identified infected hosts must be cleaned simultaneously or in rapid sequence while network containment prevents re-infection. Coordinate the cleanup across teams with a shared timeline.

Before declaring recovery, deploy preventive measures: block all identified hashes at the endpoint level, add C2 indicators to firewall and proxy blocklists, deploy SIEM detection rules for the malware's behavioral patterns, and patch the initial infection vector (email attachment, exploit).

learning_objectives: Execute the ransomware-specific incident response workflow from detection through recovery
Assess decryption options including free decryptors, backup restoration, and ransom payment considerations
Implement ransomware-specific containment to prevent encryption spread
Navigate the legal, ethical, and practical dimensions of ransom payment decisions
readings: CISA Ransomware Guide (StopRansomware.gov)
No More Ransom Project resources and decryptor database
FBI/CISA joint advisory on ransomware best practices

lecture_notes.introduction: Ransomware is the most disruptive cyber threat facing organizations today, capable of halting operations entirely within hours. Ransomware incidents require specialized response procedures because of their unique characteristics: the attacker has already achieved their objective (encrypting data), the damage is immediately visible, and a ransom demand creates time pressure and difficult decisions. Modern ransomware operations are highly professionalized, often involving initial access brokers who sell entry points, ransomware-as-a-service operators who provide the encryption tools, and affiliates who conduct the actual attacks. Double extortion adds data theft to the equation: even with backups, the attacker threatens to publish stolen data. The ransomware IR workflow prioritizes rapid scope assessment and containment to prevent further encryption, followed by a systematic evaluation of recovery options. The ransom payment decision is fraught with legal, ethical, and practical complications: OFAC sanctions may make payment illegal, payment does not guarantee decryption, and payment funds future attacks. This session provides a comprehensive framework for ransomware response.
key_concepts: Ransomware IR workflow: detect encryption activity, isolate all systems immediately, assess scope, identify variant, evaluate recovery options, execute recovery
Immediate containment: disconnect affected systems from the network, shut down unaffected critical systems preemptively, disable network shares
Scope assessment: which systems are encrypted, which are accessible but potentially compromised, which are confirmed clean
Variant identification: ransom note analysis, encrypted file extension, submission to ID Ransomware or No More Ransom
Decryption options: free decryptors (check NoMoreRansom.org), backup restoration, negotiation (last resort)
Backup assessment: verify backup integrity in isolation, check for ransomware in backup data, determine RPO gap
Payment decision framework: legal review (OFAC sanctions check), board/executive decision, insurance carrier consultation, law enforcement notification
Double extortion response: assess data exposure, prepare data breach notifications, evaluate regulatory requirements
Recovery from backup: restore to clean hardware, patch the initial access vulnerability before reconnecting, implement enhanced monitoring
Post-ransomware hardening: immutable backups, network segmentation, privileged access management, MFA enforcement
lecture_notes.demonstration: Full ransomware tabletop exercise: receiving a ransom note at 6 AM, performing scope assessment (200 of 500 endpoints encrypted, 3 of 10 servers encrypted), evaluating backup status (48-hour full backup verified clean, 12-hour incremental integrity uncertain), identifying the variant as LockBit 3.0 (no free decryptor), and working through the recovery decision framework.
setup_requirements: Ransomware scenario injects (printed ransom notes, scope assessment data)
ID Ransomware service for variant identification

review.value: The U.S. Treasury's Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) sanctions certain ransomware groups (Lazarus Group, Evil Corp, etc.). Paying ransom to a sanctioned entity is a federal crime regardless of the circumstances. Always perform an OFAC sanctions check before any payment consideration and consult legal counsel.

Never restore backups directly to production. Restore to an isolated environment first, scan for ransomware indicators (the attacker may have been in the network for weeks and backup data may contain dormant malware), verify data integrity, then deploy to clean production infrastructure.

If ransomware is actively encrypting and you cannot determine the scope, shutting down unaffected critical systems preemptively prevents their encryption. This causes planned downtime but preserves the ability to recover. The alternative — leaving them online — risks total encryption. Document the decision rationale.

Cyber insurance carriers often have pre-approved IR firms and negotiators. Engaging the carrier early ensures coverage and access to their resources. Some policies require carrier approval before payment. The carrier may also provide breach counsel and crisis communication support.

learning_objectives: Execute a data breach response including scope assessment, notification requirements, and remediation
Navigate the complex landscape of breach notification laws across jurisdictions
Coordinate response across technical, legal, communications, and executive teams
Manage customer and public communication during a data breach
readings: NIST SP 800-122: Guide to Protecting the Confidentiality of PII
State data breach notification law comparison chart
FTC data breach response guide for businesses

lecture_notes.introduction: A data breach — the unauthorized access to or exfiltration of sensitive data — triggers a cascade of legal obligations, regulatory requirements, and reputational concerns that extend far beyond the technical incident response. Unlike a malware infection that can be quietly remediated, a data breach involving personal data requires notification to affected individuals, regulatory bodies, and potentially the public. The notification requirements vary by jurisdiction, data type, and volume. A breach affecting EU residents triggers GDPR's 72-hour notification requirement. U.S. breaches trigger a patchwork of state laws each with different definitions, thresholds, and timelines. Healthcare breaches trigger HIPAA's Breach Notification Rule. The technical IR team must quickly determine what data was accessed, how many records were affected, and whether the data was exfiltrated or merely accessed. This scope assessment drives every subsequent decision: notification obligations, regulatory filings, credit monitoring offers, and public communication. Managing a data breach requires unprecedented coordination between technical, legal, communications, executive, and customer service teams, all operating under time pressure and public scrutiny.
key_concepts: Data breach vs security incident: not all incidents involve data exposure; a data breach specifically involves unauthorized access to or exfiltration of protected data
Breach scope assessment: what data types were exposed (PII, PHI, PCI, intellectual property), how many records, confirmed exfiltration vs access only
Notification trigger analysis: does the data type and jurisdiction require notification, are there safe harbor exemptions (encryption), what are the timelines
GDPR breach notification: 72 hours to supervisory authority, 'without undue delay' to individuals if high risk to rights and freedoms
U.S. state notification laws: 50+ different laws with varying definitions, thresholds (number of records), and timelines (30-90 days typical)
HIPAA Breach Notification Rule: 60 days to individuals and HHS for breaches affecting 500+ individuals, annual report for smaller breaches
SEC material incident disclosure: 8-K filing within 4 business days of determining materiality (effective December 2023)
Breach communication strategy: honest, prompt, actionable — tell people what happened, what data was involved, and what they should do
Credit monitoring and identity protection: when to offer, scope, duration, vendor selection
Regulatory investigation preparation: preserving evidence, documenting response actions, demonstrating reasonable security measures
lecture_notes.demonstration: Data breach tabletop exercise: discovering that an attacker exfiltrated a database containing 150,000 customer records (names, emails, encrypted passwords, last 4 digits of credit cards) from a SaaS application. Walking through: scope assessment, notification requirement analysis across relevant jurisdictions, drafting the customer notification letter, preparing the regulatory filing, and coordinating the public statement.
setup_requirements: Data breach notification law reference chart

review.value: Many state notification laws exempt breaches where the data was encrypted and the encryption key was not compromised. HIPAA similarly has a safe harbor for encrypted PHI. However, if the attacker obtained both the encrypted data and the key, the safe harbor does not apply. Document the encryption status of all affected data.

Engage outside breach counsel to direct the forensic investigation. IR reports prepared at counsel's direction may be protected by attorney-client privilege, preventing their use against the organization in litigation. This is critical for breach response — involve legal before producing any written findings.

Effective notification includes: what happened (plain language), what data was involved (specific fields), what the company is doing (investigation, remediation, monitoring), what individuals should do (change passwords, monitor accounts), and what resources are available (credit monitoring, helpline).

A breach affecting customers across all 50 states triggers 50+ different notification laws. Some require notification within 30 days, others 60 or 90. Some require notification to the state AG, others do not. Most organizations notify all affected individuals under the strictest applicable standard to simplify compliance.

learning_objectives: Classify DDoS attacks by type (volumetric, protocol, application layer) and respond appropriately to each
Execute DDoS mitigation using upstream filtering, CDN absorption, and rate limiting
Coordinate with ISP, CDN provider, and cloud DDoS protection services during an active attack
Distinguish DDoS distraction attacks from primary attack vectors
readings: CISA Understanding Denial-of-Service Attacks
Cloudflare/Akamai DDoS mitigation documentation
NIST SP 800-61 Rev. 2: Denial of Service incidents

lecture_notes.introduction: Distributed Denial of Service attacks overwhelm an organization's internet-facing services by flooding them with traffic from thousands or millions of compromised systems. While DDoS attacks do not directly steal data, they can cause significant financial damage through service outages, and they are frequently used as a distraction while the attacker conducts other operations such as data exfiltration or account compromise. DDoS attacks come in three main categories: volumetric attacks that saturate bandwidth (UDP flood, DNS amplification), protocol attacks that exhaust server resources (SYN flood, Ping of Death), and application-layer attacks that target specific services with apparently legitimate requests (HTTP flood, Slowloris). Each category requires different mitigation approaches. Volumetric attacks must be absorbed or filtered upstream by the ISP or CDN provider because they exceed the organization's bandwidth capacity. Protocol attacks can be mitigated with SYN cookies, connection limits, and firewall rules. Application-layer attacks require WAF rules, rate limiting, and behavioral analysis to distinguish attack traffic from legitimate users. Preparation through CDN configuration, DDoS protection service enrollment, and ISP coordination is essential because once an attack begins, it is too late to establish these relationships.
key_concepts: Volumetric attacks: UDP flood, DNS amplification, NTP amplification, memcached reflection — measured in Gbps, exceed available bandwidth
Protocol attacks: SYN flood (exhaust connection tables), fragmented packet attacks, Ping of Death — measured in packets per second
Application-layer attacks: HTTP flood (GET/POST), Slowloris (slow connections), API abuse — measured in requests per second, hardest to distinguish from legitimate traffic
Upstream mitigation: ISP null routing (blocks all traffic to target), ISP scrubbing centers, CDN absorption (Cloudflare, Akamai, AWS Shield)
On-premise mitigation: SYN cookies, connection rate limiting, geo-blocking, IP reputation filtering, WAF rules
BGP-based mitigation: Flowspec rules, BGP blackhole routing, traffic diversion to scrubbing centers
DDoS protection services: always-on vs on-demand, DNS-based routing vs BGP-based routing, capacity guarantees
Runbook execution: pre-documented steps for enabling DDoS protection, ISP contact procedures, internal communication
DDoS as distraction: attackers use DDoS to overwhelm the SOC while conducting primary operations (data theft, account compromise)
Post-DDoS analysis: attack characterization (type, volume, duration, source), infrastructure review, mitigation effectiveness assessment
lecture_notes.demonstration: Responding to a simulated multi-vector DDoS attack: initial volumetric UDP flood triggering ISP notification and CDN failover, followed by an application-layer HTTP flood requiring WAF rule adjustment, while simultaneously monitoring for secondary attack activity that the DDoS may be masking.
setup_requirements: Network monitoring tools showing traffic volume

review.value: DNS amplification exploits open DNS resolvers: a small query (64 bytes) generates a large response (up to 4000 bytes) directed at the victim via source IP spoofing. The amplification factor can exceed 50x, meaning 1 Gbps of attacker traffic generates 50+ Gbps at the target. Mitigation requires upstream filtering.

In 2013, a DDoS attack against a bank was used to distract the SOC while attackers conducted fraudulent wire transfers. Always maintain monitoring for non-DDoS threats during a DDoS incident. Assign a separate analyst to watch for secondary attack indicators while the primary team handles mitigation.

ISP null routing drops ALL traffic to the target IP — effective at stopping the flood but also blocks all legitimate traffic. Scrubbing routes traffic through a cleaning center that filters attack traffic and forwards legitimate traffic. Scrubbing maintains service availability; null routing is a last resort.

Application-layer DDoS uses valid HTTP requests, making it hard to filter. Detection relies on behavioral analysis: request rate per IP, geographic anomalies, missing expected headers (referer, cookies), unusual URL patterns, and request timing that suggests automation rather than human browsing.

learning_objectives: Investigate insider threat incidents while maintaining legal compliance and employee rights
Coordinate insider threat response across security, HR, legal, and management teams
Distinguish between malicious insider activity and policy violations or negligence
Collect and preserve evidence suitable for both internal disciplinary action and potential criminal prosecution
readings: CISA Insider Threat Mitigation Guide
NIST SP 800-53 Personnel Security controls
Carnegie Mellon CERT Insider Threat resources

lecture_notes.introduction: Insider threat incidents are fundamentally different from external attacks and require a distinct response approach. The 'attacker' is an employee, contractor, or business partner with legitimate access to systems and data, making detection harder and the response more legally and emotionally complex. Insider threats range from unintentional data exposure (an employee emailing a spreadsheet to a personal account) to deliberate sabotage or espionage. The response must balance organizational security with employee privacy rights, employment law requirements, and the potential for both civil litigation and criminal prosecution. Unlike external incident response where the goal is to remove the attacker as quickly as possible, insider threat investigation may require extended covert monitoring to understand the full scope of the activity before confronting the individual. Every action must be coordinated with HR and legal counsel. Evidence collection must follow procedures that maintain admissibility in both employment proceedings and criminal court. The emotional and cultural impact of insider threat cases requires careful handling — the investigation affects the suspect's colleagues, manager, and the broader organizational trust.
key_concepts: Insider threat categories: malicious (deliberate theft/sabotage), negligent (accidental exposure), compromised (legitimate account hijacked by external attacker)
Detection indicators: DLP alerts, abnormal data access patterns, off-hours access, large downloads, unauthorized USB usage, resignation followed by data access spike
Legal framework: employee privacy rights, monitoring policies (must be in acceptable use policy), ECPA protections, union considerations
Investigation team: security, HR, legal counsel, management chain — never investigate without HR and legal involvement
Covert monitoring: extended observation period to understand full scope before confrontation, requires legal authorization
Evidence collection: mirror employee's system image, preserve email archives, collect access logs, DLP alerts, badge records, document everything
Interview procedures: HR-led, never alone, documented, legal counsel available, aligned with employment law requirements
Preservation of employment options: investigation findings determine outcome — termination, disciplinary action, counseling, or referral to law enforcement
Criminal referral considerations: when does data theft become criminal (CFAA, trade secret theft, espionage), coordination with law enforcement
Organizational recovery: rebuilding trust, addressing systemic issues that enabled the insider threat, strengthening controls
lecture_notes.demonstration: Insider threat investigation scenario: a DLP alert shows a departing engineer downloading 2GB of proprietary source code to a personal cloud storage account 3 days after accepting a position with a competitor. Walking through the investigation steps: validating the alert, notifying HR and legal, initiating covert log review, identifying the full scope of data accessed, preserving evidence, preparing for the HR-led interview, and assessing criminal referral options.

review.value: Never investigate an employee without HR and legal involvement. Security-only investigations risk violating employment law, creating wrongful termination liability, and mishandling evidence that could be needed for prosecution. HR provides the framework for employee investigations; security provides the technical analysis.

The organization's ability to monitor employee activity depends on having an acceptable use policy that employees have acknowledged. The policy must state that company systems may be monitored, that there is no expectation of privacy on company devices, and that violations may result in disciplinary action including termination.

An employee forwarding a customer list to their personal email for weekend work is negligent. The same employee forwarding it 2 days before starting at a competitor is suspicious. The same data appearing in the competitor's marketing a month later is evidence of theft. Context determines the response.

Extended covert monitoring (watching without the employee's knowledge) reveals the full scope of activity but creates legal and ethical tensions. Duration should be the minimum necessary to understand scope. Legal counsel must approve the monitoring plan. Document the business justification for the monitoring period.

learning_objectives: Develop and execute crisis communication plans for different incident types and audiences
Manage internal communication to keep responders informed without leaking sensitive details
Prepare external communications for customers, media, regulators, and partners
Avoid common communication failures that compound incident damage
readings: Crisis Communication: A PR practitioner's guide to managing incidents
Sample breach notification communications from public incidents
SEC guidance on cybersecurity incident disclosure

lecture_notes.introduction: Communication is often the most poorly handled aspect of incident response, yet it can determine whether an incident results in a manageable disruption or a reputational catastrophe. Organizations that communicate honestly, promptly, and clearly during incidents maintain stakeholder trust. Those that delay, obfuscate, or are caught being dishonest face amplified reputational damage, regulatory scrutiny, and customer loss. Crisis communication operates on multiple channels simultaneously: the IR team needs real-time coordination, executives need status updates for decision-making, employees need enough information to avoid panic without compromising the investigation, customers need to know if their data is at risk and what to do, media need a statement to avoid speculative reporting, and regulators need timely notification to fulfill legal obligations. Each audience requires different information at different levels of detail. A failed communication strategy compounds the incident damage: delayed notification increases regulatory penalties, inconsistent messaging destroys credibility, and premature public statements before understanding the scope lead to embarrassing corrections. This session teaches the structured approach to crisis communication that successful IR programs use.
key_concepts: Crisis communication principles: be first, be right, be credible, express empathy, promote action
Internal communication tiers: IR team (full detail), executive team (decision-relevant), broader organization (need-to-know), all employees (general awareness)
External communication sequence: regulators (legal obligation), affected individuals (ethical obligation), media (public interest), partners (business relationship)
Out-of-band communication: when normal channels may be compromised, use Signal, personal phones, separate IR bridge lines
Holding statements: pre-approved templates providing minimal information while investigation is ongoing
Status update cadence: hourly for executives during active incidents, daily for broader stakeholders, event-driven for customers
Common communication failures: minimizing scope, making promises about security that cannot be kept, blaming vendors or users, delayed notification
Social media monitoring: tracking public discussion of the incident, responding to misinformation, managing narrative
Media training for spokespersons: staying on message, bridging to key points, handling hostile questions
Post-incident communication: transparency report, remediation actions taken, third-party audit results
lecture_notes.demonstration: Crisis communication exercise running parallel to a ransomware scenario: drafting the initial holding statement, writing the executive status update, preparing the customer notification, handling a media inquiry with prepared talking points, and managing a social media post from an employee that reveals sensitive incident details.
setup_requirements: Simulated media inquiries and social media posts

review.value: Communicate early even if you do not have all the facts. A holding statement saying 'We are aware of an incident and investigating' is better than silence that allows speculation to fill the void. Update as facts become available. Being first and being right are both important; balance them rather than waiting for perfection.

During the 2017 Equifax breach, employee social media posts revealed internal details before the official announcement. Establish a clear policy: during active incidents, all external communication goes through the designated spokesperson. Remind employees at the start of every incident.

Document every regulatory notification: what was communicated, to whom, when, and by what method. Regulators will evaluate not just the incident but the organization's response. Timely, honest, complete notification demonstrates good faith and may mitigate penalties.

Never promise 'this will not happen again' or 'no data was accessed.' These statements will be scrutinized and any future incident or contradicting evidence destroys credibility. Instead: 'We are implementing specific improvements including X, Y, and Z to strengthen our defenses.'

class_schedule.topic: Tabletop Exercises and IR Plan Testing
learning_objectives: Design and facilitate tabletop exercises that test organizational IR capabilities
Create realistic scenario injects that challenge decision-making across all IR phases
Evaluate exercise performance using structured assessment criteria
Translate exercise findings into IR plan improvements
readings: CISA Tabletop Exercise Packages (CTEPs)
FEMA Homeland Security Exercise and Evaluation Program (HSEEP) guide
Tabletop exercise facilitation best practices

lecture_notes.introduction: Tabletop exercises are the primary method for testing incident response plans without the cost and disruption of live simulations. A tabletop exercise gathers the IR team and key stakeholders around a simulated scenario, presenting injects (new information and developments) that require decision-making and coordination. Well-designed exercises test not just technical response but decision-making authority, communication effectiveness, and coordination between teams. The exercise designer creates a realistic scenario with planned injects that introduce complications: the initial malware detection leads to discovering a data breach, the primary backup is corrupted, the CISO is unreachable, a media outlet contacts the company, and a regulator demands information. Each inject tests a specific aspect of the IR plan and team capability. The facilitator guides discussion, ensures all participants contribute, and captures decisions and gaps. The after-action report translates exercise observations into specific IR plan improvements. Organizations that conduct regular tabletop exercises respond significantly faster and more effectively to real incidents because their teams have practiced the decision-making processes under stress.
key_concepts: Tabletop exercise design: objectives, scope, scenario development, inject timeline, participant roles, evaluation criteria
Scenario realism: base scenarios on actual incident reports, adapt known attack patterns to the organization's environment
Inject design: each inject tests a specific IR capability or decision point, injects increase complexity progressively
Participant selection: IR team, IT operations, legal, HR, communications, executive team — different exercises may emphasize different participants
Facilitation techniques: Socratic questioning, ensuring quiet participants contribute, maintaining pace, avoiding rabbit holes
Evaluation criteria: decision quality, decision speed, communication effectiveness, plan adherence, gap identification
After-action report: observations organized by IR phase, strengths, areas for improvement, specific recommendations with owners
Exercise types progression: discussion-based (tabletop), functional (partial execution), full-scale (live simulation)
Executive tabletop exercises: focused on strategic decisions (ransom payment, public disclosure, regulatory notification) rather than technical details
Exercise frequency and rotation: quarterly exercises alternating scenarios (ransomware, data breach, insider threat, supply chain compromise)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Students design tabletop exercise scenarios for each other: creating a 3-inject exercise for a supply chain compromise scenario, writing the inject descriptions, defining the expected decision points, and developing the evaluation criteria. Then each group facilitates a mini-exercise for another group.

review.value: Each inject should: (1) Be realistic and plausible, (2) Test a specific IR capability, (3) Require a decision or action, (4) Build on previous injects logically, (5) Include enough detail for discussion but not so much that it eliminates ambiguity. The best injects create disagreement among participants, revealing different assumptions.

Common failures: exercises that are too easy (no real gaps identified), too unrealistic (participants cannot suspend disbelief), dominated by one loud participant, or treated as a test (people hide weaknesses instead of learning). The facilitator must establish a learning environment, not a grading exercise.

Include executives in at least one annual exercise. They must practice making decisions about disclosure, ransom payment, and resource allocation. Executives who have never participated in an exercise make poor decisions under real incident pressure because they lack context for the tradeoffs.

Every exercise identifies gaps. Track these in a gap register with: description, severity, recommended action, owner, deadline, and status. Review the register before each exercise to verify previous gaps were addressed. An exercise program that identifies the same gaps repeatedly is failing.

class_schedule.topic: IR Plan Capstone: Full-Scale Tabletop Exercise
learning_objectives: Execute a comprehensive incident response using the IR plan developed during the semester
Demonstrate effective team coordination, decision-making, and communication under simulated pressure
Apply lessons from all previous course modules to a multi-phase attack scenario
Produce a post-incident report with metrics, root cause analysis, and improvement recommendations
readings: Review all course materials and personal IR plan
Review after-action reports from previous exercises
Prepare IR toolkit and communication templates

lecture_notes.introduction: The capstone exercise puts every skill learned this semester to the test in a multi-phase, multi-inject tabletop exercise that simulates a realistic, complex security incident. The scenario will unfold over the full class period, with new developments injected every 10-15 minutes that require immediate decisions. The exercise begins with an initial detection and escalates through containment challenges, scope expansion, executive involvement, regulatory notification deadlines, media inquiries, and recovery complications. Students will use the IR plans they developed during the semester, execute their communication templates, apply their detection and containment procedures, and demonstrate the lessons learned from previous exercises. The instructor and teaching assistants serve as the inject team, introducing complications that test specific capabilities: a key team member becomes unavailable, the SIEM goes offline during the incident, backup verification reveals corruption, a second attack vector is discovered, and a board member calls demanding a status update. The post-exercise debrief includes a structured postmortem with metrics calculation, root cause analysis, and improvement identification. The quality of the post-incident report is as important as the exercise performance itself.
key_concepts: Multi-phase scenario execution: detection, escalation, containment, scope expansion, eradication, recovery, post-incident — all in one exercise
Real-time decision-making: time-pressured decisions with incomplete information, documented with rationale
Team coordination under stress: role execution, handoffs between analysts, communication cadence maintenance
Cascading complications: injects that compound the difficulty and require adaptation of the response plan
Communication execution: drafting actual notifications, briefings, and statements during the exercise
Metrics collection during the exercise: logging decision timestamps for MTTD, MTTC, MTTR calculation
Structured postmortem: analyzing what went well, what could be improved, and deriving specific action items
Peer evaluation: constructive feedback on team members' performance, communication, and decision quality
lecture_notes.demonstration: Full capstone tabletop exercise with 8-10 injects over the class period. The scenario involves an advanced persistent threat that gained initial access through a compromised vendor VPN account, moved laterally using stolen credentials, established persistence on multiple servers, exfiltrated sensitive customer data, and deployed ransomware on a subset of systems as a distraction. Each inject introduces a new development requiring immediate response.
setup_requirements: Inject cards prepared by instructor (sealed until delivery)

review.value: The capstone exercise simulates the chaos of a real incident: incomplete information, conflicting priorities, time pressure, and unexpected complications. The goal is not to achieve a perfect response but to demonstrate sound methodology, clear communication, and effective adaptation when things go wrong.

Calculate MTTD (time from inject 1 to first detection action), MTTC (detection to containment decision), and MTTR (containment to recovery plan). Compare against industry benchmarks. These metrics demonstrate IR capability maturity and identify where the response process can be accelerated.

The post-incident report should include: incident summary, complete timeline, root cause analysis (5 Whys), detection gaps, containment effectiveness, communication evaluation, metrics, and 5-10 specific improvement actions with owners and deadlines. This report demonstrates the student's ability to learn from incidents.

This exercise closes the loop on the IR lifecycle: preparation (plan development) -> detection/response (exercise execution) -> lessons learned (postmortem) -> preparation improvement (updated plan). An effective IR program repeats this cycle continuously, with each iteration strengthening the organization's capability.

quizzes.topic: IR Frameworks, Team Structure, and Preparation

questions.question: Compare the NIST SP 800-61 and SANS incident response frameworks. For each phase in the NIST model, identify the corresponding SANS phase(s) and explain one practical difference in how each framework approaches that phase.
questions.answer: NIST Phase 1 (Preparation) = SANS Phase 1 (Preparation): Both cover the same ground. NIST Phase 2 (Detection & Analysis) = SANS Phase 2 (Identification): Both cover detection, but SANS explicitly separates identification as the determination that an incident occurred, while NIST includes more analysis depth. NIST Phase 3 (Containment, Eradication & Recovery) = SANS Phases 3, 4, 5 (Containment, Eradication, Recovery): This is the key difference — NIST groups these together as iterative activities, recognizing that you may cycle between containment and eradication multiple times. SANS separates them, providing clearer action boundaries for each phase, which is more prescriptive for less experienced teams. NIST Phase 4 (Post-Incident Activity) = SANS Phase 6 (Lessons Learned): Both cover postmortems, but NIST includes evidence retention and using incident data to improve future preparation, creating an explicit feedback loop to Phase 1.

rubric.key: Practical differences identified for each phase
Understanding of iterative vs sequential approach

questions.question: You are the new IR manager at a 500-person manufacturing company with no formal IR program. The CISO gives you 90 days to establish a basic IR capability. Describe your prioritized plan for the first 90 days, including what you would implement immediately versus what can wait.
questions.answer: Days 1-30 (Immediate): (1) Write a basic IR plan covering the top 3 incident types (ransomware, phishing/malware, data breach) with roles, escalation paths, and contact lists. (2) Identify who fills IR roles from existing staff (IT, security, legal, HR, communications). (3) Ensure basic detection is in place: verify SIEM is collecting critical logs (AD authentication, firewall, endpoint), confirm EDR is deployed on all endpoints. (4) Establish an IR communication channel (dedicated Slack/Teams channel, bridge line number). (5) Pre-negotiate an IR retainer with an external firm for surge capacity. Days 31-60 (Foundation): (6) Conduct the first tabletop exercise (ransomware scenario) to test the basic plan and identify gaps. (7) Create communication templates (internal notification, customer breach notification, regulatory filing). (8) Verify backup integrity and test restoration procedures. (9) Document the authority matrix (who can isolate systems, disable accounts, engage law enforcement). Days 61-90 (Strengthening): (10) Implement the improvements identified in the tabletop exercise. (11) Establish on-call rotation for 24/7 coverage. (12) Set up IoC sharing with relevant ISAC. (13) Schedule quarterly tabletop exercises for the next year.

rubric.key: Logical prioritization with quick wins first

questions.question: Why is it critical to capture volatile evidence (memory) before performing containment actions like shutting down a system? Provide a specific example of evidence that would be lost.
questions.answer: Volatile evidence exists only in RAM and is permanently destroyed when the system is powered off or restarted. Containment actions like shutdown, process termination, or even network isolation can modify or destroy this evidence. Specific example: An attacker running a fileless PowerShell implant that exists entirely in the memory of a PowerShell process. The malware downloads and executes code directly in memory without ever writing to disk. If the system is shut down before memory acquisition, the malware's code, its C2 configuration, the encryption keys it uses, and the data it has accessed — all evidence of the intrusion — are permanently lost. There will be no file on disk to analyze, no hash to search for, and potentially no evidence that the process existed at all (if Prefetch was disabled or the process name was legitimate). Memory acquisition using WinPMEM or LiME captures a snapshot of RAM that preserves this evidence for later analysis with Volatility.

rubric.key: Specific example with evidence that would be lost

questions.question: At 2:14 AM, your SIEM alerts on a Sigma rule detecting PsExec lateral movement: Event ID 4688 (Process Creation) shows PSEXESVC.exe spawning cmd.exe on a domain controller from source IP 10.0.5.22 (a developer workstation). Walk through your first 30 minutes of response, including specific containment decisions and their justification.
questions.answer: Minutes 0-5: Validate the alert. Check if PsExec usage is normal for this environment (it may be used by IT for management). Query EDR for full process chain on the DC: what did cmd.exe execute? Check 10.0.5.22 for the user session — is the developer logged in at 2 AM? If no legitimate session, this is highly suspicious. Declare a High severity incident. Minutes 5-10: Check the DC for the cmd.exe child processes. If they show reconnaissance (whoami, net group, nltest) or data access, escalate to Critical. Contain: disable the developer account used for the PsExec session immediately (identity containment). Do NOT isolate the DC yet — it provides authentication for the entire domain. Minutes 10-20: Investigate 10.0.5.22 (developer workstation): when did the attacker gain access, is it running malware, are other workstations compromised? Run an IoC sweep for PSEXESVC.exe execution across all endpoints. Capture memory from both the DC and workstation for forensic analysis. Minutes 20-30: Assess scope: query SIEM for all activity from the compromised account and IP. Check for additional lateral movement indicators. If scope is limited to DC + workstation, implement targeted network containment (block 10.0.5.22 from accessing other systems). If scope is broader, engage the external IR retainer. Document all actions with timestamps.

rubric.key: Correct alert validation and severity assessment
Appropriate containment balancing DC availability with security

questions.question: Explain three different containment techniques (network-based, host-based, and identity-based) for responding to an active data exfiltration incident. For each technique, describe a scenario where it is the optimal choice and a scenario where it would be counterproductive.
questions.answer: Network-based (DNS sinkhole): Optimal when the exfiltration uses DNS tunneling to a specific domain — sinkholing the domain severs the channel without alerting the attacker or disrupting the host. Counterproductive when the attacker uses direct IP connections — DNS sinkholing has no effect on IP-based C2. Host-based (EDR network isolation): Optimal when a single identified host is actively exfiltrating large data volumes and you need to stop the bleeding immediately while preserving the ability to investigate remotely. Counterproductive when the host is a shared server (file server, database) that other employees depend on — isolation causes business disruption disproportionate to the threat. Identity-based (account disable/credential revocation): Optimal when the attacker is using a single compromised service account to access data across multiple systems — disabling the account stops all activity simultaneously across all systems. Counterproductive when the attacker has established their own persistence mechanisms (web shell, implanted account, SSH keys) that do not depend on the original compromised account — disabling it alerts the attacker without stopping their access.

rubric.key: Three correct containment techniques with descriptions

questions.question: After eradicating a threat, what three specific actions would you take before reconnecting recovered systems to the production network, and why is each necessary?
questions.answer: (1) Patch the initial access vulnerability: The attacker used a specific entry point to gain initial access (phishing, unpatched vulnerability, compromised credential). If this entry point is not closed before reconnecting, the attacker (or another attacker using the same method) can immediately re-compromise the recovered systems. This is the most common cause of rapid re-compromise. (2) Deploy enhanced monitoring with aggressive alerting: Install EDR in block mode, create SIEM rules specifically targeting the attacker's known TTPs, and deploy canary files/accounts. This ensures that if eradication was incomplete and the attacker still has a foothold, their activity will be detected immediately upon reconnection rather than going unnoticed for weeks.
